Hierarchical Transformer for Motor Imagery-Based Brain Computer Interface

 << Results  
Permana Deny; Saewon Cheon; Hayoung Son; Kae Won Choi

Abstract:

In this paper, we propose a novel transformer-based classification algorithm for the brain computer interface (BCI) using a motor imagery (MI) electroencephalogram (EEG) ...Show More

Metadata

Abstract:

In this paper, we propose a novel transformer-based classification algorithm for the brain computer interface (BCI) using a motor imagery (MI) electroencephalogram (EEG) signal. To design the MI classification algorithm, we apply an up-to-date deep learning model, the transformer, that has revolutionized the natural language processing (NLP) and successfully widened its application to many other domains such as the computer vision. Within a long MI trial spanning a few seconds, the classification algorithm should give more attention to the time periods during which the intended motor task is imagined by the subject without any artifact. To achieve this goal, we propose a hierarchical transformer architecture that consists of a high-level transformer (HLT) and a low-level transformer (LLT). We break down a long MI trial into a number of short-term intervals. The LLT extracts a feature from each short-term interval, and the HLT pays more attention to the features from more relevant short-term intervals by using the self-attention mechanism of the transformer. We have done extensive tests of the proposed scheme on four open MI datasets, and shown that the proposed hierarchical transformer excels in both the subject-dependent and subject-independent tests.
Published in: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics ( Volume: 27, Issue: 11, November 2023)
Page(s): 5459 - 5470
Date of Publication: 14 August 2023

ISSN Information:

PubMed ID: 37578918
DOI: 10.1109/JBHI.2023.3304646

Funding Agency:

Contents
SECTION I.

Introduction

The brain-computer interface (BCI) provides a direct control channel from a human brain to a computer device. The motor imagery (MI), which is the mental imagination of the motoric movement, is a popular BCI paradigm since it enables a human to spontaneously control the physiological brain signal readable by a scalp electroencephalogram (EEG) [1]. A brain signal is caused as a result of the potential changes around the brain area responsible for a specific motoric control. Therefore, each MI intention of a different limb movement such as a left hand, right hand, or feet, incurs a different brain signal that is read by an EEG device and decoded to an intended MI by a classification algorithm. However, the design of an accurate MI classification algorithm is still considered as a very challenging research topic because of the intractable variability in the environmental and physiological conditions affecting the EEG signal characteristics across time and subjects.

Recently, deep learning techniques have emerged as a new trend for designing the MI classification algorithm. The deep learning algorithms have greatly outperformed traditional approaches, such as the common spatial pattern (CSP) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA), in most benchmark datasets in terms of the classification accuracy. The majority of the existing deep learning techniques for MI classification handle the spatial and temporal features by using the convolutional neural network (CNN) and recurrent neural network (RNN). For example, the studies in [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7] show that a CNN can explicitly learn temporal and spatial features. A class of RNN, named long short-term memory (LSTM), has been used for extracting the temporal features in [8], [9], while the hybrid of CNN and LSTM has been proposed in [10]. However, CNN and RNN are known to have their own limitations. CNN has spatial invariance, which means that CNN treats all spatial locations in the same manner. Therefore, the discrepancy in the signal characteristics according to EEG sensor locations cannot be properly reflected by CNN. On the other hand, RNN has a problem in maintaining a long-term memory and propagating gradients backwards through time to old time steps. This is a big drawback since relating long-term brain signals over time is a key to enhance the performance of the MI classification. Therefore, the exploration to find a deep neural network architecture more suitable to an EEG MI task is of great importance.

The transformer is a prominent deep learning model that was first introduced by [11] in 2019 for the natural language processing (NLP) application. Since then, the transformer has been considered as a de facto standard for the NLP task with its incomparable performance, and used as a backbone for the state-of-the-art NLP models such as the bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) and generative pre-trained transformer (GPT). The transformer relies on an attention mechanism that learns the contextual relationship between sequential data segments in different positions by weighing the significance of data segments and combining them according to the weights. For the past few years, the transformer has successfully widened its application area to domains other than the NLP. For example, the vision transformer (ViT) [12] and its successors outperform the state-of-the-art CNN models with much lower computational overhead in the computer vision domain. In another example, AlphaFold 2 [13], which uses the transformer architecture at its core, has shown an astounding performance in a protein structure prediction task.

In spite of the great success of the transformer in various fields, the adoption of the transformer in the BCI application has relatively been slow. In [14], the EEG of the imagined speech has been classified by the combination of CNN and the transformer. However, the algorithm in [14] is not applied to the MI classification task. The study in [15] has proposed to use the transformer for the self-supervised learning from the datasets for different EEG tasks. Although the downstream task of the MI classification has been evaluated in [15], the performance is quite low (i.e., 42.6 % in the BCI competition IV dataset 2a). The transformer is used for classifying the MI intention in [16] [17]. However, in [16], the classification accuracy is only 68.54 % for the four-class MI in the PhysioNet MI dataset while the state-of-the-art model shows the accuracy of at least 80 %. From these works, we can see that only preliminary studies with non-optimized performance are presented for the transformer architecture for the MI classification. Therefore, further investigation into the MI BCI by the transformer is required to fully realize the potential of the transformer.

In this paper, we propose a novel hierarchical transformer architecture for the MI classification algorithm. We utilize the attention mechanism of the transformer to mainly address the variability of the MI EEG signals in the time domain. Since the period of one MI trial is long (e.g., 4 seconds), a subject can imagine multiple motor tasks in an unpredictable pattern or easily lose a focus on the intended MI task. In addition, some time periods can be corrupted by the artifacts caused by environmental or physiological interference. Therefore, it is important to give more attention to the time periods during which the intended motor task is imagined without artifacts. We attempt to achieve this goal by means of the attention mechanism of the transformer. We use the two-level hierarchical transformer in which the low-level transformer extracts features from short-term intervals and the high-level transformer gives more attentions to more relevant features.

We have conducted extensive tests of the proposed algorithm to verify its performance on four publicly available MI datasets, i.e., BCI competition IV dataset 2a [18], PhysioNet MI dataset [19], Cho dataset [20], and Lee dataset [21]. The accuracy of the proposed algorithm is evaluated with the subject dependent test with the 10-fold cross-validation (10F-CV) setting. In addition, the subject independent test with the leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (LOSO-CV) setting has been conducted to show the generalization capability of the proposed algorithm. We show that the proposed algorithm outperforms most of the existing MI classification algorithms in terms of the accuracy in all test settings in all benchmark datasets. We also show the proposed hierarchical transformer pays more attention to more relevant time periods by plotting the attention weights. The source code for evaluating the proposed algorithm is available at [22].

Our contribution is summarized in the following.

  • We propose a novel MI classification strategy based on the hierarchical transformer. The proposed algorithm can resolve the problem of temporal variability in EEG signals by paying more attention to relevant time intervals.

  • The proposed method has been extensively evaluated in four different MI datasets. The results show that the proposed method achieves a state-of-the-art performance in the subject-independent setting as well as the subject-dependent setting.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section II explains the motivation for the hierarchical transformer architecture and gives a detailed description of the proposed transformer model. Section III presents the classification performance and attention weights of the proposed algorithm along with the explanation on the benchmark datasets, transformer model parameters, and training and evaluation methods. Finally, the paper is concluded in Section IV.

SECTION II.

Proposed Hierarchical Transformer Model

A. Motivation for Hierarchical Transformer Architecture

The protocol commonly used in MI experiments (e.g., [18], [19], [20], [21]) involves subjects to sit down on a comfortable chair, wait for a random cue, and perform MI during a certain period of time. The period during which one MI intention (e.g., the movement of right/left/both hands, feet, or tongue) is imagined by a subject is called a trial. As shown in the description of a trial in Fig. 1(d), a trial begins with a cue that gives the MI instruction to the subject via a screen. The subject is supposed to imagine the instructed MI intention during the trial period. The EEG device records multi-channel signals from multiple electrodes, which capture the brain activity caused by the MI.

Fig. 1.

Illustration of MI experimental paradigm during a single trial: (a) illustration of imagined hand movement, (b) ERS and ERD patterns, (c) attention weight from the hierarchical transformer model, (d) experiment steps.

Suppose that the EEG device has Nch channels and records Ttrial samples during one trial period. The EEG signal from the ith trial is represented by

ei={eic,t}c=1,…,Nch, t=1,…,Ttrial∈RNch×Ttrial,(1)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {e}}^{i} = \lbrace e_{c,t}^{i}\rbrace _{c=1,\ldots,N_{ch},\ t=1,\ldots,T_{trial}} \in \mathbb{R}^{N_{ch}\times T_{trial}}, \tag{1} \end{align*} where eic,t denote the EEG signal of the cth channel at the tth sample of the ith trial.

For training and evaluation, each trial can be tagged with a label that represents the MI intention. Let bi denote the label of the ith trial. Each possible MI intention is mapped to a class represented by a number. Let us denote by Nclass the number of classes, i.e., the number of possible MI intentions. Then, bi can have a value among 1,…,Nclass. The target of the MI decoding algorithm is to estimate the most probable bi based on ei.

The EEG device captures collective activities from a group of a large number of neurons. If groups of neurons are synchronized in response to an event, the amplitude of the EEG signal at specific frequency bands increases because of the superposition of the synchronized signals. This phenomenon is called an event-related synchronization (ERS). On the other hand, the decrease in the amplitude of the EEG signal due to the out-of-sync signals from neurons is called a event-related desynchronization (ERD). The ERS and ERD patterns over time and frequencies are important clues for classifying the MI intention.

The period of one trial in existing MI datasets is typically long, i.e., around 4 seconds. Within one trial, the start point and duration of actual MI period showing the appropriate ERS and ERD pattern can be different from trial to trial. In addition, the period of one trial is long enough to make a subject imagine multiple isolated motor tasks, which can cause irregular and discontinuous ERS and ERD patterns, as shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b). Even if subjects are instructed to repetitively perform the MI task for the continuous ERS and ERD patterns, subjects can easily be distracted during long experimental sessions. The uncertainty in the position of the actual MI period and multiple discontinuous MI periods can be an obstacle in enhancing the MI decoding performance.

In Fig. 2, we show the time-frequency plots of EEG signals of trials selected from the BCI competition datasets 2a. In this figure, we can see that multiple discontinuous ERS and ERD patterns appear in irregular positions during a single trial. In addition, there might be some time periods with irrelevant signals in between the periods with intended MI. Putting these irrelevant signals into the MI decoding algorithm can incur the risk of erroneous classification.

Fig. 2.

Example time-frequency plots from the BCI competition dataset 2a. The EEG channel is C3.

One way to resolve this problem is to utilize the attention mechanism that is widely used in the deep learning framework. If the attention mechanism is used, the algorithm is trained to selectively give more attention to the signals and features that contribute more to enhancing the performance. With the attention mechanism, the MI decoding algorithm can focus on the time period during which an actual MI happens, while rejecting irrelevant signals.

In this paper, we use the transformer for incorporating the attention mechanism into the MI decoding algorithm. Differently from the mixture of the attention mechanism and other types of neural networks (e.g., CNN), the transformer is built purely based upon the attention mechanism at its core.

We divide the whole trial period into short-term intervals. A short-term interval is short enough to include EEG signals from only a single and consistent MI state. The low-level transformer (LLT) is used for extracting features from all short-term intervals within a trial. Then, some features from the short-term intervals with the intended MI state have useful information while the other features can have irrelevant information. The high-level transformer (HLT) receives the features from the LLT as inputs, and pays more attention to the features with useful information by assigning higher attention weights to them, as shown in Fig. 1(c). Those features with high attention weights are combined together by the self-attention mechanism of the transformer to produce accurate classification results.

In summary, in the proposed hierarchical transformer architecture, the LLT can extract a feature for each short-term interval corresponding to a single MI state, and these features can be combined with the attention mechanism by using the HLT.

B. Transformer

In this subsection, we will explain the general concept of the transformer as a prerequisite for the proposed hierarchical transformer model. It is noted that, although the standard transformer employs both the self- and cross-attention modules in the encoder and decoder structures, we will explain only the self-attention module in the encoder.

The transformer is given in Fig. 3, which illustrates the flows and tensor dimensions. The transformer consists of L layers, each of which contains the multi-head self-attention and position-wise feed-forward neural network (FFNN) sublayers. The residual connection and layer normalization are applied at the end of each sublayer. Starting from the input of the transformer, we will explain each sublayer one by one.

Fig. 3.

Transformer architecture including (a) transformer encoder, (b) multi-head self-attention, and (c) scaled dot-product attention.

Suppose that the number of the input tokens is Ntoken and the embedding dimension (i.e., the length of a token) is D. Let xini∈RD denote the ith input token of size D for i=1,…,Ntoken. In addition, xin∈RNtoken×D denotes the tensor of all input tokens generated by stacking the input tokens along the first dimension. The positional encoding can be applied to the input tokens for embedding the positional information of the token. In this paper, we use the learnable positional encoding, denoted by p∈RNtoken×D. Then, the output of the positional encoding module is

xpos=xin+p.(2)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{pos}={\bf {x}}^{in}+{\bf {p}}. \tag{2} \end{align*} Now, we focus on the lth layer of the transformer. The input and output of the lth layer of the transformer is denoted by xl,in and xl,out, respectively. The input of the first layer is x1,in=xpos, and the input of the lth layer for l=2,…,L is the output of the (l−1)th layer, i.e., xl,in=xl−1,out.

Let us explain the multi-head attention sublayer, which applies the attention mechanism in multiple heads in parallel. The number of the heads is denoted by Nhead. If we suppose that D is divisible by Nhead, we can define the embedding dimension for each head as Dhead=D/Nhead. The weight matrices for the query (WQ), key (WK), and value (WV) are the model parameters with the shape of

WQ∈RD×Dhead,WK∈RD×Dhead,WV∈RD×Dhead,(3)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {W}}^{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times D_{head}}, {\bf {W}}^{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times D_{head}}, {\bf {W}}^{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{D\times D_{head}}, \tag{3} \end{align*} The weight matrices for the query, key, and value of the nth head in the lth layer are given by Wl,Qn, Wl,Kn, and Wl,Vn, respectively. Then, the query, key, and value, which are Ntoken×Dhead tensors, are computed as
Qln=xl,inWl,Qn,Kln=xl,inWl,Kn,Vln=xl,inWl,Vn.(4)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {Q}}_{n}^{l}={\bf {x}}^{l,in}{\bf {W}}^{l,Q}_{n},\quad {\bf {K}}_{n}^{l}={\bf {x}}^{l,in}{\bf {W}}^{l,K}_{n},\quad {\bf {V}}_{n}^{l}={\bf {x}}^{l,in}{\bf {W}}^{l,V}_{n}. \tag{4} \end{align*} The attention matrix for the self-attention, denoted by Aln∈RNtoken×Ntoken, is calculated based on the query and key as
Aln=softmax(Qln(Kln)TDhead−−−−−√),(5)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathbf {A}_{n}^{l}=\mathop {\text{softmax}}\bigg (\frac{{\bf {Q}}_{n}^{l} ({\bf {K}}_{n}^{l})^{T}}{\sqrt{D_{head}}}\bigg), \tag{5} \end{align*} where the softmax operator is applied along the last dimension. The (i,j)th component of the attention matrix represents the weight for the value of the jth input token for constructing the ith output token. Then, the output of the nth head of the multi-head attention module, denoted by xl,attn∈RNtoken×Dhead, is
xl,attn=AlnVln.(6)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{l, att}_{n} = \mathbf {A}_{n}^{l}{\bf {V}}_{n}^{l}. \tag{6} \end{align*} The outputs from all heads are concatenated as
xl,att=concat(xl,att1,…,xl,attNhead)Wl0,(7)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{l, att} = \mathop {\text{concat}}({\bf {x}}^{l, att}_{1}, \ldots, {\bf {x}}^{l, att}_{N_{head}}) {\bf {W}}^{l}_{0}, \tag{7} \end{align*} where xl,att∈RNtoken×D, the concat operator is applied along the last dimension, and Wl0∈RD×D is the weight matrix for combining the outputs from multiple heads. Finally, the output of the multi-head attention sublayer is derived by making a residual connection and applying the layer normalization as follows.
xl,MHA=layer_norm(xl,in+xl,att),(8)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{l, MHA} = \mathop {\rm{layer\_{n}orm}}({\bf {x}}^{l,in} + {\bf {x}}^{l, att}), \tag{8} \end{align*} where the layer_norm operator normalizes the vectors along the last dimension. The layer_norm operator subtracts the mean from the vector and divides the vector by the standard deviation, and then multiply and add some learnable model parameters.

The next sublayer is the position-wise FFNN, which is the series of two fully-connected layers applied to each token separately. The equation for the FFNN sublayer is

xl,ff=GELU(xl,MHAWl,ff1+bl,ff1)Wl,ff2+bl,ff2,(9)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{l, ff} = \mathop {\text{GELU}}({\bf {x}}^{l, MHA}{\bf {W}}^{l,ff}_{1}+\mathbf {b}^{l,ff}_{1}) {\bf {W}}^{l,ff}_{2} + \mathbf {b}^{l,ff}_{2}, \tag{9} \end{align*} where ‘GELU’ is the GELU activation function and Wl,ff1∈RD×Dff, bl,ff1∈RDff, Wl,ff2∈RDff×D, and bl,ff2∈RD are the weights and biases, and Dff is the hidden dimension. Finally, the FFNN sublayer is completed by the residual connection and layer norm, and the output of the lth layer is given by
xl,out=layer_norm(xl,ff+xl,MHA).(10)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{l, out} = \mathop {\rm{layer\_{n}orm}}({\bf {x}}^{l, ff} + {\bf {x}}^{l, MHA}). \tag{10} \end{align*} The output of the whole transformer is equal to the output of the last layer, that is, xout=xL,out. The transformer receives the input xin∈RNtoken×D and generates the output xout∈RNtoken×D of the same shape. Then, the transformer can be represented by the following function.
xout=Transformer(xin).(11)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{out}=\mathop {\text{Transformer}}({\bf {x}}^{in}). \tag{11} \end{align*}

C. Proposed Hierarchical Transformer Model

Now, we explain the proposed hierarchical transformer model. The overall flowchart of the proposed method is given in Fig. 4, and the hierarchical transformer model is shown in Fig. 5 in more detail. We use the transformer in Section II-B as a building block for the proposed model. The proposed model consists of two transformers, one is the LLT and the other is the HLT.

Fig. 4.

Flowchart of proposed hierarchical transformer.

Fig. 5.

Proposed hierarchical transformer architecture.

We will first explain the tokenization process in Section II-D that processes the EEG signals to generate tokens. There are multiple LLTs, each of which corresponds to one short-term interval and produces the feature from the EEG signals within that short-term interval. A number of tokens corresponding to a short-term interval are fed into the LLT. The LLT will be explained in detail in Section II-E. The HLT takes the features from the LLTs and combines them with the attention mechanism. We will explain the HLT in Section II-F. The hierarchical training strategy will be explained in Section II-G.

D. Tokenization

We explain the tokenization process to generate the tokens fed into the LLT. The only preprocessing step applied to the raw EEG signal is the bandpass filter since the other preprocessing procedure such as the independent component analysis (ICA) has not improved the performance of the proposed method. The bandpass filtered EEG signal in one trial is given by

e={ec,t}c=1,…,Nch, t=1,…,Ttrial∈RNch×Ttrial,(12)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {e}} = \lbrace e_{c,t}\rbrace _{c=1,\ldots,N_{ch},\ t=1,\ldots,T_{trial}} \in \mathbb{R}^{N_{ch}\times T_{trial}}, \tag{12} \end{align*} where the trial index i is removed from (1) to focus on a single trial.

We equally divide the EEG signal e into K tokens, each of which has Ttoken EEG samples. Suppose that Ttrial is divisible by Ttoken, and we have

K=Ttrial/Ttoken.(13)
View Source \begin{align*} K=T_{trial}/T_{token}. \tag{13} \end{align*} The EEG signal for the kth token is given by
etoken,k={ec,t}c=1,…,Nch, t=(k−1)Ttoken+1,…,kTtoken∈RNch×Ttoken,(14)
View Source \begin{align*} \begin{split} {\bf {e}}^{token,k} &= \lbrace e_{c,t}\rbrace _{c=1,\ldots,N_{ch},\ t=(k-1)T_{token}+1,\ldots,kT_{token}} \\ &\in \mathbb{R}^{N_{ch}\times T_{token}}, \end{split} \tag{14} \end{align*} for k=1,…,K.

The kth token is a one-dimensional signal made by flattening etoken,k such that

ok=flatten(etoken,k)∈RD,(15)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathbf {o}^{k}=\mathop {\text{flatten}}({\bf {e}}^{token,k})\in \mathbb{R}^{D}, \tag{15} \end{align*} where the flatten operator flattens a multi-dimensional tensor into an one-dimensional tensor, and D
D=Nch⋅Ttoken(16)
View Source \begin{align*} D=N_{ch}\cdot T_{token} \tag{16} \end{align*} The tokens, ok for k=1,…,K, are directly fed into the LLT without any additional feature extraction stage. Although the feature extraction methods such as the phase space trajectory matrix (PSTM) [23], principal component analysis (PCA), or common spatial pattern (CSP) can be applied first, we do not use such methods since they have not improved the performance of the proposed algorithm.

E. Low-Level Transformer (LLT)

The LLT is a transformer as explained in Section II-B. For the LLT, the number of layers is LLLT, the number of the tokens is NLLTtoken, the embedding dimension is D, the number of the heads for the multi-head attention is NLLThead, and the hidden dimension for the FFNN is DLLTff.

Multiple LLTs are applied within a single trial, and each LLT takes an input from the respective short-term interval. Each LLT takes NLLTtoken tokens as an input. The input tensor to the ith LLT, denoted by xin,LLT(i)∈RNLLTtoken×D, is generated by stacking the tokens

ok′sfork=⌊(i−1)(NLLTtoken/2)⌋+1,…,⌊(i−1)(NLLTtoken/2)⌋+NLLTtoken.(17)
View Source \begin{align*} {\mathbf {o}^{k}}'{\rm s \, for }\,k=&\lfloor (i-1)(N_{token}^{LLT}/2)\rfloor +1,\\ &\ldots,\lfloor (i-1)(N_{token}^{LLT}/2)\rfloor +N_{token}^{LLT}. \tag{17} \end{align*} From (17), we can see that the input tokens to the consecutive LLTs overlap. The number of the LLTs within a trial is denoted by NLLT, which is decided so that the index of the last token of the NLLTth LLT does not exceed the number of tokens in a trial (i.e., K).

Similar to (11), we can define Transformer_LLT that stands for the function for the LLT, and the output of the ith LLT is given by

xout,LLT(i)=Transformer_LLT(xin,LLT(i)),(18)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{out,LLT(i)}=\mathop {\rm{Transformer\_{L}LT}}({\bf {x}}^{in,LLT(i)}), \tag{18} \end{align*} where xout,LLT(i)∈RNLLTtoken×D is the output from the ith LLT. It is noted that the parameters of all LLTs are shared, that is, the weights and biases of all LLTs are the same.

The output xout,LLT(i) has NLLTtoken tokens along the row. We apply the global average pooling to xout,LLT(i) for compressing it into a single token.

yLLT(i)=1NLLTtoken∑j=1NLLTtokenxout,LLT(i)j∈RD,(19)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathbf {y}^{LLT(i)}=\frac{1}{N_{token}^{LLT}}\sum _{j=1}^{N_{token}^{LLT}} {\bf {x}}_{j}^{out,LLT(i)}\in \mathbb{R}^{D}, \tag{19} \end{align*} where xout,LLT(i)j∈RD is the jth row of xout,LLT(i).

The result of each LLT, yLLT(i), is not only an input to the HLT, but also able to be used for the classification. Recall that the number of classes (i.e., the number of possible MI intentions) is denoted by Nclass. The classification head is a simple linear layer such that

pLLT(i)=softmax(yLLT(i)Wcls,LLT),(20)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {p}}^{LLT(i)} = \mathop {\text{softmax}}(\mathbf {y}^{LLT(i)}{\bf {W}}^{cls,LLT}), \tag{20} \end{align*} where pLLT(i)=[pLLT(i)l]l=1,…,Nclass∈RNclass is the classification result of the ith LLT, Wcls,LLT∈RD×Nclass is the weight matrix for the classification of the LLT, and yLLT(i) is treated as a row vector. The lth component of pLLT(i) (i.e., pLLT(i)l) can be interpreted as the probability of class l.

F. High-Level Transformer (HLT)

There is a single HLT for a trial, which further processes the output from the LLTs. The HLT is a transformer with the number of layers LHLT, the number of the tokens NHLTtoken, the embedding dimension D, the number of the heads for the multi-head attention NHLThead, and the hidden dimension for the FFNN DHLTff.

The HLT takes the outputs of the LLTs as an input. In addition to this, the HLT takes a learnable class token τ∈RD as an input as well. Then, the number of the tokens for the HLT is NHLTtoken=NLLT+1. Let xin,HLT∈RNHLTtoken×D denote the input of the HLT. The jth row (i.e., token) of xin,HLT, denoted by xin,HLTj∈RD, is given as

xin,HLTj={τyLLT(j−1)if j=1,if j=2,…,NHLTtoken.(21)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}_{j}^{in,HLT}= {\begin{cases}\boldsymbol{\tau } & \text{if }j=1,\\ \mathbf {y}^{LLT(j-1)} & \text{if }j=2,\ldots,N_{token}^{HLT}. \end{cases}} \tag{21} \end{align*} The class token τ is used for combining the information from the outputs of the LLTs (i.e., yLLT(1),…,yLLT(NLLT)) by using the attention mechanism.

The output of the HLT, denoted by xout,HLT∈RNHLTtoken×D, is obtained by the function Transformer_HLT, which is the transformer corresponding to the HLT. That is,

xout,HLT=Transformer_HLT(xin,HLT).(22)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {x}}^{out,HLT}=\mathop {\rm{Transformer\_{H}LT}}({\bf {x}}^{in,HLT}). \tag{22} \end{align*} We only consider the first row of xout,HLT, which corresponds to the class token, as an output. Then, the output of the HLT is
yHLT=xout,HLT1∈RD,(23)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathbf {y}^{HLT} = {\bf {x}}_{1}^{out,HLT}\in \mathbb{R}^{D}, \tag{23} \end{align*} where xout,HLTj∈RD is the jth row of xout,HLT.

For the HLT, we apply the similar classification head to that of the LLT as in the following.

pHLT=softmax(yHLTWcls,HLT),(24)
View Source \begin{align*} {\bf {p}}^{HLT} = \mathop {\text{softmax}}(\mathbf {y}^{HLT}{\bf {W}}^{cls,HLT}), \tag{24} \end{align*} where pHLT=[pHLTl]l=1,…,Nclass∈RNclass is the classification result of the HLT and Wcls,HLT∈RD×Nclass is the weight matrix for the classification of the HLT. The classification result of the whole hierarchical transformer is the class with the maximum probability in pHLT. That is, the final classification result is
b^=l=1,…,NclasspHLTl.(25)
View Source \begin{align*} \hat{b}=_{l=1,\ldots,N_{class}} p^{HLT}_{l}. \tag{25} \end{align*} We can refer to the attention matrix inside the HLT to see the attention weight on each short interval. Let AHLT,ln∈RNHLTtoken×NHLTtoken denote the attention matrix at the nth head of the lth layer inside the HLT. Please, see (5) for the definition of the attention matrix of a transformer. The (i,j)th component of AHLT,ln is denoted by AHLT,ln,i,j. Then, aHLT,ln=[AHLT,ln,1,j]j=2,…,NHLTtoken∈RNLLT is the attention weight from the outputs of all LLTs to the class token. We take an average of aHLT,ln over all heads and layers to calculate
a¯HLT=1LHLTNHLThead∑l=1LHLT∑n=1NHLTheadaHLT,ln∈RNLLT.(26)
View Source \begin{align*} \bar{\mathbf {a}}^{HLT}=\frac{1}{L^{HLT}N_{head}^{HLT}}\sum _{l=1}^{L^{HLT}}\sum _{n=1}^{N_{head}^{HLT}}\mathbf {a}_{n}^{HLT,l}\in \mathbb{R}^{N_{LLT}}. \tag{26} \end{align*} We will later plot a¯HLT to visualize the attention mechanism.

G. Training Strategy and Loss Function

Now, we explain the training strategy of the proposed hierarchical transformer. The one-hot expression of the ground truth label is denoted by B={Bl}l=1,…,Nclass∈RNclass. With the one-hot expression, only one component of B, which corresponds to the true MI intention, is one, and the other components are zero. To solve the overconfidence problem, we can use the label smoothing technique [24] that converts the hard one-hot labels into the soft ones.

We conduct the hierarchical training that separately trains the LLT and HLT in sequence, instead of simultaneous training. First, we train the LLT with the following cross entropy loss function.

LLLT=−∑i=1NLLT∑l=1NclassBllog(pLLT(i)l),(27)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathcal{L}_{LLT}=-\sum _{i=1}^{N_{LLT}}\sum _{l=1}^{N_{class}} B_{l} \log (p^{LLT(i)}_{l}), \tag{27} \end{align*} where pLLT(i)l is the classification result from the LLT defined in (20). With this loss function, the LLT is trained to predict the true label only based on the EEG signals within a short-term interval. Then, the trained LLT produces an informative feature about each short-term interval, which can be utilized by the HLT.

Once the LLT is trained, the parameters of the LLT are frozen for training the HLT. The HLT is trained to minimize the following cross entropy loss function.

LHLT=−∑l=1NclassBllog(pHLTl).(28)
View Source \begin{align*} \mathcal{L}_{HLT}=-\sum _{l=1}^{N_{class}} B_{l} \log (p^{HLT}_{l}). \tag{28} \end{align*} In the training of the HLT, the HLT learns to give higher attention weights to the more important short-term intervals, and to optimally combine the features from the LLTs to maximize the classfication performance.

SECTION III.

Experimental Results and Discussion

A. Dataset

In this study, we evaluate the proposed hierarchical transformer algorithm by using four different MI datasets, as shown in Table I. These datasets are a large-scale open datasets that are well known among MI researchers, which include BCI competition IV dataset 2a [18], PhysioNet MI dataset [19], Cho dataset [20], and Lee dataset [21]. We have downloaded the datasets from a third-party platform called the mother-of-all-BCI-benchmarks (MOABB) [25]. The MOABB provides a pipeline to obtain many famous BCI datasets and aims to improve transparency and reproducibility in the BCI research.

TABLE I Dataset Description. In the MI Class Column, ‘EC’, ‘RH’, ‘LH’, ‘BH’, ‘f’, ‘t’ Stand for Eye-Closed, Right-Hand, Left-Hand, Both Hands, Both Feet, and Tongue MI Intentions, Respectively

Each dataset has different number of EEG channels, sampling rate, number of subjects, MI classes, and number of trials per subject. In Table I, we summarize these parameters for characterizing all datasets. In this table, Nsubj and Ntps denote the numbers of subjects and trials per subject, respectively. Henceforth, we will call each dataset by its code as shown in Table I.

Dataset I (i.e., BCI competition IV dataset 2a) is the most famous MI dataset. With its high number of trials per subject, this dataset is suitable for studying the MI classification for an individual subject. However, it is difficult to use this dataset for the subject-independent test due to a small number of subjects. Dataset II (i.e., Physionet) has a large number of subjects, and potentially provides insights into the development of the subject-independent algorithm. Since some subjects (i.e., subjects 87, 88, 90, 92, 97, and 100) do not have full attributes, we exclude them from the analysis, leaving this dataset with 103 subjects. Dataset III (i.e., Cho dataset) and Dataset IV (i.e., Lee dataset) are two-class MI datasets with a large number of subjects. In Dataset III, we exclude subjects 32, 46 and 49 since their data are corrupted, leaving Dataset III with 49 subjects.

The model parameters for the proposed hierarchical transformer are presented in Table II. The selection of the model parameters is done through a hyperparameter tuning procedure. For the hyperparameter tuning, we have used the grid and Bayesian searches as well as a manual trial and error procedure. For Datasets I, II, and IV, one trial lasts 4 seconds since the start of the cue (see Fig. 1). On the other hand, a trial of Dataset III lasts 3 seconds. The rest of the model parameters are carefully selected so that the classification accuracy is maximized.

TABLE II Model Parameters for Hierarchical Transformer for Each Dataset

The input EEG signal is e={ec,t}c=1,…,Nch, t=1,…,Ttrial∈RNch×Ttrial as given in (12). Therefore, the dimension of the input EEG signal for each trial is given by Nch×Ttrial, which is the number of channels times the number of samples per trial. As shown in Table II, the dimensions of the input EEG signals for Datasets I, II, III, and IV are 22×1000, 64×640, 64×1536, and 62×1000, respectively.

Our primary objective in evaluating the proposed model in different datasets with various attributes is to assess the generalization capabilities of the model over EEG data with different characteristics. While most of the other motor imagery studies typically test their classification methods on at most two datasets, we have done the test on four different datasets. Therefore, the performance and generalizability of the proposed method are more thoroughly evaluated in this paper.

B. Training and Validation Methods

The experiments have been conducted in a PyTorch environment with the graphical processing unit (GPU) of Tesla V-100 having a 32 GB memory. We have separately trained the LLT and HLT for 5000 epochs. For training, we have utilized the Adam optimizer with the learning rate of 0.0005.

We use two test methods, one is the subject-dependent test and the other is the subject-independent test, for dividing the training and test sets as shown in Fig. 6. The subject-dependent test takes the training and test sets from the same subject. Therefore, with the subject-dependent method, the neural network is trained for each individual subject and it can only be used for the same subject in the inference stage. Since the neural network has to be trained for each new subject, the usability of the trained neural network is rather limited.

Fig. 6.

Subject-dependent and subject-independent training and test methods.

On the other hand, in the subject-independent test, the test set is from a specific subject, while the training set is formed by combining data from all the other subjects. With this method, the neural network is trained by data from multiple subjects at the same time for generalizability, and therefore, can be used for different subjects without re-training.

The 10-fold cross-validation (10F-CV) is used for the subject-dependent test, and the leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (LOSO-CV) is used for the subject-independent test. We define ei,j∈RNch×Ttrial for i=1,…,Nsubj and j=1,…,Ntps as the EEG signal for the jth trial of subject i.

Suppose that we evaluate subject i∗ by using the subject-dependent test with 10F-CV. The Ntps trials of subject i∗ (i.e., ei∗,j for j=1,…,Ntps) are randomly divided into 10 folds of equal sizes. One fold is selected as the test set, and all other folds are used for training. Then, we have 10 performance results, each of which is the result of selecting one fold out of 10 folds as the test set. The performance of subject i∗ is obtained by averaging these 10 results. Since the trained model parameters are different for each subject, the training should be done for each new subject in the subject-dependent method.

There is a tradeoff in deciding N for the N-fold cross-validation. As N gets smaller, the test set becomes larger and the test results are more credible. On the other hand, a larger N leads to a larger training set, which ensures that the deep learning model is better trained. We believe that increasing the size of the training set by setting a larger N is more important in the EEG study. It is because the size of the EEG dataset is generally smaller than that of other domains (e.g., vision or NLP) while an advanced deep learning model requires a large dataset for training. Furthermore, the robustness of the test results of the N-fold cross-validation is better than the fixed division of training and test sets since the test is done N times with N different divisions. Therefore, we have chosen a sufficiently large N=10.

C. Classification Accuracy Comparison

We can test the generalization capability over subjects by using the subject-independent test with LOSO-CV. If we select one subject i∗, the test set consists of the Ntps trials of subject i∗ (i.e., ei∗,j for j=1,…,Ntps), and the training set consists of the (Nsubj−1)×Ntps trials of subject i's for i≠i∗ (i.e., ei,j for i≠i∗ and j=1,…,Ntps). The generalization capability can be proved by this method since no trial from the same subject is used for training. If the classification algorithm has a good performance in this subject-independent test, it is likely that a new subject can be accommodated by the same model without further training.

We show the classification performance of the proposed algorithm in comparison to the previous works in Table III. We have evaluated the proposed algorithm with all four datasets by using both the subject-dependent and subject-independent methods. Our algorithm is compared to the previous works using the same dataset and training/validation method. Since some previous works have used only a part of available subjects, we have included the number of subjects involved in training and validation in this table.

TABLE III Classification Accuracy of the Proposed Method Compared to Previous Studies

The previous works in Table III can be categorized into two approaches: the first approach is to combine traditional feature extraction and machine learning, and the other approach is the use of a pure end-to-end machine learning algorithm. The former category includes the studies in [3], [4], [5], [6], [10], [20], [31], [32], [36], [38], [39], [40], which mainly resort to the multivariate analysis for feature extraction, and the study in [34] which utilizes an EEG source imaging (ESI). On the other hand, the latter category, which is the end-to-end machine learning, includes the studies in [2], [7], [8], [29], [33], [37], [41].

Now, we will take a look at the results from each dataset one by one. In the subject-dependent test of Dataset I, the proposed algorithm achieves the accuracy of 0.900, while the previous studies in [27], [4], [10], [17], and [26] yield 0.826, 0.819, 0.800, 0.747, and 0.746, respectively. Note that some studies differently utilize trials marked with ‘E’ as evaluation dataset and ‘T’ as training dataset, such as study in [2], [10], [17], and [26] but this method is still considered as a subject-dependent method. We can see that the proposed algorithm significantly outperforms other previous algorithms.

In the subject-independent test of Dataset I, our algorithm achieves the accuracy of 0.703. The study in [2] has proposed five different neural network architectures, among which the best one is ‘ShallowConvNet’ with the accuracy of 0.395. In addition, the algorithm in [30] achieve the accuracy of 0.681. Although our algorithm has the best accuracy, the accuracy is rather low because of the limited number of subjects.

Let us move on to the accuracy results of Dataset II. This dataset is renowned for having a large number of subjects. Since there are many subjects, some works have not included all subjects in the study as seen in Table III. First, we compare the results of the subject-dependent test. Here, our algorithm achieves 0.835 with 103 subject used for training and validation. The three best previous results are 0.925 by [34] with 14 subjects, 0.794 by [31] with 20 subjects, and 0.682 by [8] with 12 subjects. On the other hand, other studies that make use of a larger number of subjects yield relatively lower performance, e.g., 0.657 by [33] with 109 subjects and 0.512 by [32] with 99 subjects. Even if the performance of the proposed algorithm is worse than that of ESI-CNN in [34], the performance of ESI-CNN is not rigorously verified by a large number of subjects.

In the subject-independent test of Dataset II, the accuracy of our algorithm is evaluated as 0.802 with 103 subjects. In comparison, the result reported in [29] is 0.640 with 105 subjects. It is noted that the accuracy of the subject-independent test of Dataset II is much higher than that of Dataset I thanks to a large number of subjects, which facilitates the generalization across subjects.

Dataset III also has a large number of subjects (i.e., 52) and only has two MI classes (i.e., LH and RH). The accuracy of the proposed algorithm in the subject-dependent test is 0.846 with 49 subjects, which outperforms the results from the existing works, for example, 0.770 by [36] with 10 subjects, 0.688 by [9] with 49 subjects, and 0.674 by [20] with 49 subjects. We could not find any existing result by the subject-independent test of Dataset III. In this test, the proposed algorithm have achieved 0.834 with 49 subjects.

We move on to Dataset IV including 54 subjects with two MI classes. In the subject-dependent test, our algorithm achieves 0.821 with 54 subjects while three best results from previous studies [40], [42], and [39] are 0.747, 0.743, and 0.732, respectively with 54 subjects. Recently, Dataset IV has actively been used for studying the subject-independent paradigm. The accuracy of our algorithm in the subject-independent test of Dataset IV is 0.813 with 54 subjects, which outperforms the accuracy of 0.747, 0.744, 0.742, 0.735, and 0.720 respectively reported by [40], [42], [7], [41], and [37] by a very wide margin.

In Table III, there are transformer-based algorithms such as S3T [27], t-CTrans [35], and MAtt [17]. Among them, S3T [27] and t-CTrans [35] directly applies the transformer architecture. On the other hand, MAtt [17] primarily focuses on mapping EEG features onto the Riemannian symmetric positive definite (SPD) manifold by leveraging the calculation of a sample covariance matrix. Then, the attention mechanisms are employed within the SPD manifold. We can see in Table III that the proposed algorithm outperforms other transformer-based algorithms.

In summary, the proposed algorithm shows an excellent performance compared to other existing works in terms of the classification accuracy in both the subject-dependent and subject-independent tests of all datasets.

D. Confusion Matrices

The confusion matrix provides insight into the classification performance of each individual MI class. In Fig. 7, we show the confusion matrices of the proposed algorithm, which are obtained by the subject-independent tests of all four datasets. These confusion matrices are normalized for each ground truth label. In addition, Table IV shows the precision, recall, and F1 scores of each MI class for all datasets. The precision measures the accuracy of a given predicted MI class, while the recall shows how much portion of samples belong to a specific MI class are successfully predicted. The F1 score is the combination of the precision and recall by a harmonic mean.

TABLE IV Precision, Recall, and F1 Score
Fig. 7.

Confusion matrices from the subject-independent test of four datasets. ‘EC’, ‘RH’, ‘LH’, ‘BH’, ‘F’, ‘T’ stand for eye-closed, right-hand, left-hand, both hands, both feet, and tongue MI intentions, respectively.

It is clearly shown by the confusion matrices in Fig. 7 that the classification performances of all MI classes are unbiased. Table IV also indicates that the performance disparity among each MI class is low, and the precision and recall are well balanced.

E. Subject Evaluation Result

This subsection reports the classification performance for each individual subject when the subject-independent test is used. Fig. 8 shows a box plot depicting the minimum, maximum, median, first quartile, and third quartile of the recall scores of all MI classes for each subject.

Fig. 8.

Subject evaluation results from the subject-independent test of four datasets.

Fig. 8(a) depicts the accuracy of all subjects in Dataset I. The average and standard deviation of the accuracy over subjects are 0.703 and 0.144, respectively. The standard deviation is rather large since we have subjects with very high accuracy (e.g., subjects 1, 8, and 9) while some other subjects have poor performances (e.g., subjects 5 and 6).

The subject evaluation results of Dataset II is shown in Fig. 8(b). In Dataset II, our algorithm achieves the average accuracy of 0.802 with standard deviation of 0.046. Fig. 8(c) and (d) show the subject evaluation results of Datasets III and IV, respectively. In Dataset III, the average and standard deviation of the accuracy of the proposed algorithm are 0.834 and 0.061, respectively, while those are 0.813 and 0.083 in Dataset IV, respectively.

We can see that the standard deviation of the accuracy is much smaller in Datasets II, III, and IV in comparison to that in Dataset I. The main reason is that the generalization capability over subjects is limited in Dataset I due to the limited number of subjects.

F. Attention Weight

In this subsection we show the attention weight of the proposed hierarchical transformer architecture. In many transformer-based researches, the attention weight is typically used for visualizing the internal mechanism of the model by showing the tokens to which the model focuses its attention on (e.g., [12]). As written in Section II-F, in the HLT, the class token τ combines the information from the outputs of the LLTs (i.e., yLLT(1),…,yLLT(NLLT)) by using the attention mechanism. The weight given to the information from each short-term interval is the attention weight, which is derived from the attention matrix inside the HLT. The attention weight of the HLT, denoted by a¯HLT∈RNLLT, can be calculated according to (26).

First, we will show that the attention mechanism inside the HLT is essential in enhancing the classification performance. To prove that, we collect the token yLLT(i) and the corresponding attention weight (i.e., the ith component of a¯HLT) from the LLT. We then rank yLLT(i) based on attention weights in ascending and descending orders. In this test, the HLT takes the first N tokens from the sequence of tokens in ascending and descending orders to make a decision. Then, the HLT that takes tokens from the sequence in the descending order makes a decision based on the tokens with higher attention scores. The result is provided in Fig. 9(a). In this figure, it is evident that the accuracy of the descending order is better than that of the ascending one. This proves that the attention score actually marks the importance of each token in predicting the MI intention. Now, we visualize the attention weight a¯HLT to show how much attention is given to each short-term interval. It is noted that the time duration of the short-term interval of Datasets I, II, III, and IV are 0.16, 0.1875, 0.09375, and 0.12 seconds, respectively. The short-term intervals are half-overlapped, and the numbers of short-term intervals (i.e., NLLT) of Datasets I, II, III, and IV within one trial are 49, 41, 63, and 65, respectively.

Fig. 9.

Performance comparison of different number of NHLTtoken token on ascending order and descending order w.r.t. attention weight.

We present some examples of attention weights including the corresponding EEG signals and time-frequency plots in Fig. 10. Note that we use the EEG signals from channel ‘C3’ for this figure. In Dataset I, the trials with unacceptable artifacts are marked as rejected trials. In Fig. 10, we show two example normal trials and two example rejected trials. In Fig. 10, the attention weight at the bottom is described by the intensity of a red color. From the time-frequency plots, we can see the ERS and ERD patterns, which indicate the increase and decrease in power in a specific band due to motor imagery. The frequency bands most relevant to the motor imagery task are known to be mu (7–12 Hz) and beta (12–30 Hz).

Fig. 10.

Example attention weights along with the corresponding EEG signals and time-frequency plots for normal trials and rejected trials.

The normal trials in Fig. 10(a) shows the relationship between the attention weights and ERS/ERD patterns. We can see in Fig. 10(a) that the time periods during which high attention weights appear coincide with the periods where significant mu and beta-ERD is observed in the time-frequency plot. In the rejected trials in Fig. 10(b), we can clearly see the existence of artifacts, i.e., a large fluctuation in the amplitude. In these figures, we can see that the proposed hierarchical transformer assigns low attention weights to the short-term intervals affected by signal artifacts.

Now, we demonstrate how the attention mechanism can effectively reject noise in the EEG signal by artificially injecting a noise. The original trial without noise is depicted in Fig. 11(a). We inject a white noise into an MI trial over 0–1.2 s as shown in Fig. 11(b) in which the noisy period is marked by a red rectangle. Upon observing Fig. 11(a), we can see that the attention is initially given to the period 0–1.2 s. However, once the noise is injected, the attention becomes very weak. These figures clearly illustrate the capability of the attention mechanism to identify and reject the noise.

Fig. 11.

Noise injection experiment.

From now on, we explain the attention weight for trials and subjects. In Fig. 12, we show some example attention weights of trials from subject 1 in Dataset 1. We have randomly selected five example trials for each MI class. In addition, we present the attention weight averaged for each MI class, and the global attention weight averaged over all trials of subject 1. The average of attention weights highlights the trends in the time spots where the model focuses its attention on for each subject. We can observe that the attention is focused onto the beginning of a trial for the LH and RH classes. On the other hand, the attention weights of the F and T classes tend to be more dispersed over a trial. In general, the global attention weight shows that higher attention weights are given to the beginning of a trial.

Fig. 12.

Example attention weights of trials from subject 1 in Dataset I. Five trials are randomly selected for each MI class. ‘F’, ‘LH’, ‘RH’, and ‘T’ stand for both feet, left-hand, right-hand, and tongue MI classes, respectively.

In Fig. 13, we show the attention weights averaged over all trials for each subject. In all datasets, it is commonly seen that the attention weight is low at the very start of a trial, and it increases and reaches the peak before the first quarter of a trial. We believe the reason for this is that a subject tends to perform an MI task right after a cue is given. A short low-attention period at the beginning of a trial is caused by the delay until a subject responds to a cue. Then, a subject performs the first MI task, the timing of which is consistent over different subjects for the same dataset. After the first imagination, a subject may try to keep performing MI tasks during a trial, but its timing and frequency are irregular, which results in relatively lower average attention weights during the remaining part of a trial.

Fig. 13.

Average attention weights for each subject.

In one dataset in Fig. 13, the patterns of the attention weights of all subjects are similar besides some exceptions. However, we can see clear differences in the patterns of attention weights for different datasets. For example, the attention weights of Dataset III are very low until the first one-fifth of a trial period, while the high attention weights are found earlier in other datasets. In addition, the attention weights of Dataset IV has sparser patterns compared to those of other datasets. We believe that this discrepancy is caused by the differences in experimental setups, for example, the EEG recording device, the timing and presentation of a cue, experimental environment, and instructions to subjects.

G. Ablation Study

In this subsection, we discuss the effect of different training strategies and hyperparameters on the performance via an ablation study. Let us recall our strategy for training the proposed hierarchical transformer model in Section II-G. The hierarchical transformer consists of LLT and HLT, each of which is separately trained one by one. The LLT is trained first by minimizing the loss for the LLT (i.e., LLLT), and then the HLT is trained by minimizing the loss for the HLT (i.e., LHLT) while the parameters of the LLT are frozen. We call this strategy the hierarchical training.

Before selecting this strategy, we have conducted an ablation study to compare the performances of the hierarchical, simultaneous, and hybrid training strategies. The simultaneous training strategy trains the LLT and HLT at the same time by minimizing LHLT. On the other hand, the hybrid training strategy trains the LLT first by minimizing LLLT until the parameters of the LLT converge, and then trains the LLT and HLT at the same time by minimizing LHLT without freezing the parameters for the LLT.

In Table V, we present the results of the ablation study for selecting the training strategy. In this table, we also have added the results for the ablation study on the number of EEG samples for a token (i.e., Ttoken). We can see in this table that the hierarchical training strategy with Ttoken=4 performs the best.

TABLE V Ablation Study on Training Strategy for Subject 1 of Dataset I
SECTION IV.

Conclusion

In this paper, we have proposed a hierarchical transformer to solve the MI classification problem for BCI. The proposed hierarchical transformer consists of the LLT, which extracts features from short-term intervals, and the HLT, which combines the features from all LLTs to produce the classification result. We have shown that the proposed algorithm has an excellent performance in both the subject-dependent and subject-independent tests for four different datasets. The attention weights of the HLT are interpreted as the importance of each short-term interval in making a final classification. We have shown that more attention is given to more relevant short-term intervals.

In summary, the proposed algorithm achieves noteworthy performance results and provides interpretable outcomes such as attention weights, which promotes the potential application of the transformer to general EEG studies as well as BCI.

In future work, we will extend the proposed hierarchical transformer architecture for continuous MI decoding [43]. Thanks to the attention mechanism, the proposed model is inherently suitable for continuous MI decoding. The attention module in the transformer can combine the information from the past EEG tokens in a causal way. Furthermore, we can also extend the model to incorporate a Bayesian estimation concept such as the sequential probability ratio test (SPRT) or change detection so that more advanced continuous MI decoding is realized.
